Organic Chemistry I Lectures CHEM-C 341, Fall 2020
Week 11 Lecture Slides – Week of 11/2/2020
Instructor: Kasey J. Clear

Two mechanisms of nucleophilic substitution
Both give the same overall reaction, but the mechanisms are unique

Or let some cats explain the difference
S N 1
S N 2
Leaving group leaves, carbo cat ion forms, nucleophile attacks
Leaving group leaves at the same time as nucleophile attacks

Why does the mechanism matter?
Both HBr and PBr 3 can convert an alcohol into an alkyl bromide

S N 2 rate equation
Rate equation:
Rate depends on:
Typically ___________ bases react faster as nucleophiles in S N 2 reactions
Typically ___________ bases make better leaving groups in S N 2 reactions

S N 1 rate equation
Rate equation:
Rate depends on:
Typically ___________ bases react faster as nucleophiles in S N 1 reactions
Typically ___________ bases make better leaving groups in S N 1 reactions
Is it S N 1 or S N 2?

Practice
Classify the following molecules as 1°, 2° or 3°

More on the S N 1 reaction
Carbocation stability is the most important factor the S N 1 reaction rate

Factors affecting carbocation stability
Structure of a carbocation:

Factors affecting carbocation stability
1. Alkyl substituents
S N 1 rates with tertiary structures:
- fast with good leaving groups (e.g. halides) - fast with leaving groups such as –OH or –OR with acid catalyst
- slow when carbocation cannot be stabilized

2. Delocalization with adjacent C=C π system
“allylic” carbocations
“benzylic” carbocations
3. Delocalization with adjacent lone pair
Examples of stable carbocations
Examples of actual reaction rates

Practice
Which would have the fastest and slowest S N 1 reaction?
Which would have the fastest and slowest S N 1 reaction?

Week 11 Lecture Slides – Week of 11/2/2020
Instructor: Kasey J. Clear

More on the S N 2 reaction
Stability of the transition state is the most important factor the S N 2 reaction rate
Needed: 1) easy approach of Nu to carbon 2) stabilization of charges in TS

Steric effects in S N 2 reactions
Rate increases as the number of substituents and bulkiness decreases
Especially fast rates for methyl and primary compounds

Electronic effects in S N 2 reactions
Adjacent C=C and C=O π systems increase the reaction rate
allylic/benzylic systems
α -carbonyl systems
Examples of S N 2 reaction rates

Practice
Which would have the fastest and slowest S N 2 reaction with I ─ ?
Which would have the fastest and slowest S N 2 reaction with I ─ ?
